title: COBIT
lead: Governance and management framework for enterprise IT — used to establish control objectives and align IT with organisational goals.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body: COBIT (Control Objectives for Information and Related Technologies) is a framework developed by ISACA for the governance and management of enterprise IT. Now in its 2019 edition, COBIT provides a comprehensive set of governance and management objectives organised around 40 processes, covering everything from strategic alignment and value delivery to risk management and resource optimisation. It is widely used in regulated industries and by organisations seeking to establish clear IT governance structures.

sections.heading: ITOM Perspective
sections.body: COBIT addresses the governance layer of an IT Operating Model — the 'who decides what' and 'how do we ensure IT delivers value and manages risk'. While frameworks like ITIL focus on operational processes and Agile focuses on delivery, COBIT provides the overarching governance structure that ensures IT is aligned with business objectives, risks are managed, and resources are used responsibly. In ITOM engagements, COBIT is particularly relevant when designing governance bodies, decision rights, and control frameworks.

items.desc: COBIT clearly separates governance (evaluate, direct, monitor) from management (plan, build, run, monitor) — a critical distinction in ITOM design
structured across 5 domains: EDM (governance), APO, BAI, DSS, MEA (management)
11 factors (enterprise strategy, IT role, threat landscape, etc.) that tailor COBIT implementation to organisational context
0–5 maturity scale based on CMMI, used to assess and target process maturity
mechanism linking enterprise goals → IT-related goals → governance/management objectives, ensuring traceability
the governance domain; sets direction, monitors compliance, evaluates outcomes
strategic planning, architecture, innovation, risk, and portfolio management
solution development, change management, asset management, and transition
operations, service requests, incident management, and continuity
performance monitoring, internal controls, and regulatory compliance
IT Governance assessment — evaluate current governance maturity using COBIT as benchmark
Governance model design — define decision rights, governance bodies, and escalation paths
COBIT-ITIL integration — align governance (COBIT) with operational processes (ITIL) in a coherent operating model
Compliance framework design — build control frameworks for regulated environments
Risk management integration — embed IT risk management into the operating model governance layer
